I think the inheritance here is problematic. By having the Async classes inherit from the sync ones, we're breaking LSP ([Liskov Substitution Principle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Liskov_substitution_principle)), and it's also not following the base `MCServer` class, which specifies that for example the `status` method needs to be synchronous.

If we wanted to do this properly, we'd need a `SyncMCServer` and `AsyncMCServer` classes, which `SyncJavaServer`, `AsyncJavaServer`, `SyncBedrockServer` and `AsyncBedrockServer` whould need to inherit from. Alternatively, we could have `MCServer` base that only defines shared things across both sync and async versions, and let the individual classes inherit from that.

As much as I'd like to do this, I'm not sure I like this kind of structure, and the proper structure (above) would be kind of big and annoying to deal with. I'm not entirely sure that this is a good change to make, next time it may be a good idea to make an issue before making a PR, so that we can discuss whether this kind of change will even make sense.

I would however like to hear @kevinkjt2000's opinion here, I'm not completely against doing this, but not if it means breaking LSP and the base `MCServer` not actually being correct type-wise with the async classes.

Hi unfortunately this is not the correct way to implement multisite in django.
If you want to do that you have to set 
```
SITE_ID = None
```
In your settings file and not leave it out completely :D
That will make django deduce the site from the request instead of reading it from a file.
So if you where settings SITE_ID from code in your settings, that is not the correct way to do it, you should never modify settings after the application has loaded ;)

`def __init__(self, dags: Union[DagHandle, Dict[str, DagHandle]]):`

If a single DAG is passed, it works as currently. If a dict is passed, it's of the form `route: DAG`. This makes it much more obvious how the routes map to the sub-dags.

First of all, so exciting to see you back around here @dmontagu! 🤩🎉

## PR Review

Now to the PR review:

I've been reviewing this for the last couple of days. Very clever all around. Amazing job, as always! 🚀

I added a couple of comments here and there. A couple of opinions in the places where I suspect my opinion could be relevant.

## Type Checks from VS Code - PyRight

I noticed a couple of errors with VS Code/PyRight in some places, I think mainly about getting a dict key form a dict where it is not required, or things like that.

Would it be valuable to point them out and suggest the fixes or is it just too much noise on top of the actual PR review?

I'm just adding a couple of those comments for now.

## Questions

About some of the questions in the description, my opinions inline.

Pretty much in favor with everything, for the rest I don't have a strong opinion or enough knowledge.

**Things left to resolve:**

* [x] Field kwargs
	* [x] Should we remove or keep the 'examples' kwarg on `Field`?
		* If not, what is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Re-add the examples keyword argument
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. All things equal, +1 to re-adding it. Unless there's something against it.
	* [x] Should we remove or keep the 'const' kwarg on `Field`? (This was used to indicate a field was required and the only allowed value was its default.)
		* If not, what is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop this argument in favor of `Literal` and/or validators as appropriate (i.e., keep as is on main branch)
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. If it's the same, I would think it's better to keep one single way to do it, and if there's now a "standard" way to do it with type annotations, I would use that. One single way reduces combinatorial logic, maintenance, testing. And it's easier for users to learn one single thing than two, even more if it's two competing ones.
	* [x] Should we change the `regex` kwarg to `pattern` in the `Field` signature?
		* **Tentative plan:** Change `regex` to `pattern` (i.e., keep as is on main branch)
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. As JSON Schema uses the same `pattern` keyword, I would vote for that. `regex` might be more intuitive from Python-land, but for me, `regex` is more ambiguous as to if it takes a regular expression object or a pattern string. ## kwargs in `Field()`

Details about asking for no more kwargs in `Field()`:

I would request to **not** accept any extra kwargs. I asked for that here in this hidden comment: https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/4516#discussion_r1010870226

Copying here for context/completeness:

> This `**extra` has been used to add stuff to JSON Schema.

> But in some cases, people pass other stuff here expecting it to be used later or for other purposes, but then it also affects JSON Schema.

> I would like to ask to make it a single keyword argument `extra` instead of `**extra`, or alternatively, to have another single argument `json_schema_extra` so that extensions for JSON Schema can be apart from extra values passed here for other purposes.

> I haven't seen a use for this apart from JSON Schema, and the fact that it makes `Field()` accept any keyword arguments makes editors not complain about arguments that actually do nothing. I've seen this sometimes, e.g. recently with a `Field(required=True)`, but `required` is not a valid argument, if it's required or not is defined by the absence of a `default`. But the code seemed fine and linters passed, because it was taken by the catch all `**extra`.

> It's a breaking change, but I think it's totally worth it and this is the right time for those changes.

## Semi-off topic, highlights

**Note**: no action item here, just praise and highlights of non-obvious extra benefits. 🎉

Very clever implementation! 🚀

I like how the core schema refs, def refs, and json refs are stored and correlated, and then updated when necessary, but still stored. And how everything is stored in the object.

I think this will allow me to do something I've been wanting to do in FastAPI, to pre-generate the JSON Schema stuff for each endpoint, and when there's an error, to show the specific endpoint with an error instead of just saying "something is wrong".

## Wishlist - collect errors

I was checking the wishlist of things I wanted for Pydantic and almost everything is already done. 🎉

From the things not there yet, there's one single thing I would like to ask that is related to JSON Schema, although not sure if it could be a subsequent PR. It's also related to the point above.

### The problem

First the problem.

Currently, an example like this, rightfully breaks:

```Pyhton
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict


class Car:
    def __init__(self, make: str, model: str, year: int):
        self.make = make
        self.model = model
        self.year = year

class Model(BaseModel):
    f1: int = 1
    f2: Car

    model_config = ConfigDict(arbitrary_types_allowed=True)


Model.model_json_schema()
```

But if `Model` is in a FastAPI path operation, and maybe `Car` is in a sub-model in a sub-field, in a large app, when calling the OpenAPI endpoint the whole thing will break, saying there's a problem with `Car`, but without saying where exactly... and more importantly, none of the OpenAPI will be generated. So, a single broken field breaks the whole OpenAPI and Swagger UI.

### Feature Request

Currently it immediately raises an error like:

```
Cannot generate a JsonSchema for X
```

...that stops the rest of the JSON Schema generation.

I would like instead to accumulate those errors and get them all together, but to be able to retrieve the rest of the JSON Schema.

### Possible Implementation

I think it's better to state the problem in case you see a better way to solve it, but I can also imagine possible implementations. For example:

```Python
schema = Model.model_json_schema()
```

...raises as normally, but:

```Python
schema, errors = Model.model_json_schema(keep_errors=True)
```

Doesn't raise, and returns a JSON Schema where anything with an error is replaced with `{}` and `errors` is a list of stored exceptions. Of course, `keep_errors` could use some bikeshedding on the parameter name, the same for the return interface, but you get the idea.

It could also be a context manager, maybe too complex, but that would keep the API interface (params, return) of `model_json_schema()` unmodified for both cases, don't know. And it would probably only be used by FastAPI and libraries like that, so not too bad on that side. But it would require contextvars and all that dance. E.g.

```Python
with ErrorCollector as c:
	schema = Model.model_json_schema()
print(e.errors)
```

Again, the specific implementation is up for discussion, but I would like to have a way to do this.

### Plan in FastAPI

My plan is also later to put this in FastAPI in a way that (if I manage to) when there's an error like this, the traceback would use Rich and point to the right file and line with the specific FastAPI *path operation* that uses that model. But I think that to be able to do that I first need to pre-compute and collect the JSON Schema errors per path-operation, and not only at the global level.

## Breaking Changes

About the breaking changes, mostly renaming things, in the small few places this happens, I think this is the right moment to do it and discard the cruft of backwards compatibility for those things.

An improvement in that migration, alternative to keep backwards compatibility, would be a tool to automatically update code to use the new things, for the simple ones (I can imagine, e.g. moving kwargs in `Field()` to `json_schema_extra`, or changing `regex` to `pattern`). Maybe that could also work for the `model_config`.

I haven't done it, but I know it's possible and feasible with LibCST. @Kludex has built a couple of CLI tools that do that type of thing, e.g. https://github.com/Kludex/bump-testclient

Hey Brian,

I checked the full history of `taskcluster-proxy` in the monorepo, and `taskcluster-proxy` in its original repository, and this appears never to have been the behaviour of `taskcluster-proxy` in the past.

TLDR; I think this is the wrong place to make the fix.

I think whatever broke, broke somewhere else, and this fix may end up causing us more pain than it solves. For example, it looks like this will set the `Content-Type` header for requests that have no body, such as GET requests, which could introduce adverse affects in middleware or caching proxies etc. I think passing through only the headers that are sent, plus the Authorization header, is the correct approach, and I suspect whatever was calling taskcluster-proxy is the thing that broke, rather than taskcluster-proxy itself.

I realise this might sound like I'm splitting hairs, but it feels like this change will mask problems elsewhere and could lead to unexpected results. For example, calling code that doesn't set the header when it runs through the proxy, wouldn't work if it was pointed at the taskcluster endpoint directly. The clients should all set the `Content-Type` header when it is required, so if a client is broken and no longer setting it, we have a different problem that this change would mask, but would still exist if the same client was used calling a taskcluster service directly.

I think the next steps for us is to do a deep dive to understand what is not passing the `Content-Type` header in a request to the proxy (whether it is something in a task, something in docker-worker, or something in a taskcluster client) and hopefully we can isolate the component that introduces the issue, and find the commit that broke it, and hopefully find the smoking gun to show what caused this issue, and fix it there. ☁️ 🔫

Looks ok. As far as I see there are a couple of use cases we would like to cover:

- When we release the package to PyPI we call `sentinelhub.config --clear` and then upload the package. So if `config.json` wouldn't exist then `sentinelhub.config --clear` would trigger its creation. Therefore `config.json` will always be released together with the package and anyone who istalls it with pip will have it.
- If a developer clones a git repo and install the package e.g. with `pip install -e .` then `config.json` won't be created automatically. But often during development it's convenient to have it so that you can just copy-paste credentials into it.

Because of the 2nd case I suggest adding the following function into `setup.py`

```Python
def try_create_config_file():
    """ After the package is installed it will try to trigger saving a config.json file
    """
    try:
        from sentinelhub.config import SHConfig
        SHConfig()
    except BaseException:
        pass
```
and calling it at the end after the `setup` is called. I checked that this then `config.json` automatically right after a package is installed.

looks great overall! just a couple of questions/nits

one broader questions i want to clarify is what assumptions we are making when accessing the payload of the request object.

if the request is a form request and contains a form-encoded body, the following calls will return
```python
request.get_data(as_text=True) # reads from the stream, caches the body, and returns it
request.form                   # parses the form data from the cache and returns the form data
request.get_data(as_text=True) # returns the cached body (will contain the original form-encoded body!)
````

whereas calling:
```
request.data                   # reads from the stream, parses the form data, caches the form data and caches the remaining body (which is now empty, and will return `""`)

request.get_data(as_text=True) # reads and returns the cached body (which is empty)
request.form                   # returns the cached form data
request.get_data(as_text=True) # reads and returns the cached body (which is empty)
```

see here for a detailed explanation: https://github.com/pallets/werkzeug/blob/bb21bf90b0b121e3ed45b9950b823e4b43a81fd8/src/werkzeug/wrappers/request.py#L385-L390

so that the parser will potentially behave differently depending on previous calls to `request.data` or `request.get_data(parse_form_data=True)`.
i'm not sure exactly how to address this.

after writing all this and thinking a bit more about the werkzeug API, i think it may be the best call to NOT call `get_data` at all, and just rely on the properties `request.data`, `request.values`, `request.form`, `request.args`, etc, which seems to be the way it was intended.
you lose the bit of convenience that `as_text=True` provides, but at then the behavior will be consistent.

**Design question:** At the API level, could we consolidate `prefetch_blocks` and `async_fetch_batch` with a single `prefetch_batches` argument that gives the number of batches to asynchronously prefetch?

## Motivation

I think this will be a lot cleaner from an end-user perspective, with a single argument controlling prefetching behavior that has clear batch-based semantics. We ultimately care about **pipelining upstream batch preparation with downstream batch computation**, and both prefetching batches over the network and background batch slicing/concatenation/formatting fall under the former, so having a single batch-based argument controlling how many batches we want to prepare while a single batch is being processed seems like better UX. IMO, the object store transfer prefetching should probably be an implementation detail in facilitating a generic "how many batches to we want to prepare in the background on this worker" API, similar to the semantics of the [`tf.data` prefetching API](https://www.tensorflow.org/api_docs/python/tf/data/Dataset#prefetch).

## Do We Need Two Knobs?

Since this seems like nicer UX, the only reasons that we'd want to have two knobs (that I can think of) are:
1. We want to have different levels of prefetching for object store transfers compared to background slicing/concatenation/format conversion.
2. The prefetching unit for the two args should be different (e.g. batch vs. block).

(1) could be true. If we assume that we're not hitting NIC bandwidth or CPU bottlenecks during deserialization or batch prep, then prefetching object store transfers is ultimately limited by the node's object store capacity, while background slicing/concatenation/format conversion is ultimately limited by the node's worker heap, which are separate resource pools with different capacities. This is further complicated by format conversions leading to memory inflation. E.g. for a Pandas batch format UDF on Arrow blocks executed on a node that has equal object store and worker heap allocations, you could imagine that we want to prefetch 8 batches to the object store, but only do 1 background batch slice and format conversion since the Pandas format conversion results in a DataFrame that's 4x larger than the Arrow data (and the UDF will need to at least allocate an output in the worker heap as well).

However, this may be a non-issue if:
* the Python worker heap is typically the bottleneck rather than the node's object store (I think this is true for transformations/trainers that don't use a NumPy/Arrow narrow waist), or;
* we can rely on Ray Core's object pull manager to do proper backpressure on object transfers (I believe this is true).

The user can set `prefetch_batches` tailored to whatever eliminates compute resource stalls subject to a Python worker heap cap, and object transfer prefetching will do a best-effort fulfillment of `prefetch_batches`, subject to Ray Core's backpressure on object transfers when the node's object store pressure gets high. I'm thinking that this might make (1) a non-issue.

(2) is the status quo, but I think that the current object store prefetching API would be better expressed in terms of batches (this has been a long-standing TODO), so I don't think that (2) is a concern.

## Semantics and Implementation

For `.map_batches()`, this `prefetch_batches` arg would have the existing `async_fetch_batch` semantics, except that the buffer size is configurable. Down the line, we might have this arg control the number of blocks that are prefetched in the actor compute model.

For `.iter_batches()`, this `prefetch_batches` arg would have the existing `async_fetch_batch` semantics along with exposing the buffer size, _and_ would control how many blocks are prefetched into the object store on the consumer node. Namely, if we know the number of rows in a block from the readily-available block metadata (this should be defined in the common case), we would prefetch `N` blocks such that `N` is the smallest number of blocks that satisfies `prefetch_batches`, i.e. we choose an `N` such that `sum([b.num_rows for b in blocks[:N]) >= prefetch_batches * batch_size` and `sum([b.num_rows for b in blocks[:N-1]) < prefetch_batches * batch_size`. If the block metadata doesn't have the number of rows in the block (e.g. the dataset is a lazy read of CSV files, for which we don't know the number of rows until the entire files has been read), then we can prefetch one block at a time.

What do y'all think? cc @jiaodong

Old: New: I propose we keep the radius calculation for the x-offset, but remove the 45deg angle (e.g. `LABEL_COSINE` is 1 and no y-offset is applied). @stuhood and I have been thinking that at least for Python for now, we stick with one target per BUILD file. I did not push back on this change for Terraform because it the atoms are directory-based, not file-based. So you need less granular overrides.

import pickle


class UnpickableException(RuntimeError):
    def __init__(self, message: str):
        super(UnpickableException, self).__init__(message)

    def __reduce__(self):
        raise TypeError("Can't pickle this exception")


exception_blob = None
try:
    raise UnpickableException("Fail")
except Exception as e:
    exception_blob = pickle.dumps(e)

print("Exception blob:", exception_blob)

import time
import socket
import re
import select
import errno
import os
import sys
import platform

import subprocess as ssubprocess
import sshuttle.helpers as helpers
from sshuttle.helpers import log, debug1, debug2, debug3, get_env

POLL_TIME = 60 * 15
NETSTAT_POLL_TIME = 30
CACHEFILE = os.path.expanduser('~/.sshuttle.hosts')

# Have we already failed to write CACHEFILE?
CACHE_WRITE_FAILED = False

SHOULD_WRITE_CACHE = False

hostnames = {}
queue = {}
try:
    null = open(os.devnull, 'wb')
except IOError:
    _, e = sys.exc_info()[:2]
    log('warning: %s' % e)
    null = os.popen("sh -c 'while read x; do :; done'", 'wb', 4096)


def _is_ip(s):
    return re.match(r'\d+\.\d+\.\d+\.\d+$', s)


def write_host_cache():
    """If possible, write our hosts file to disk so future connections
       can reuse the hosts that we already found."""
    tmpname = '%s.%d.tmp' % (CACHEFILE, os.getpid())
    global CACHE_WRITE_FAILED
    try:
        f = open(tmpname, 'wb')
        for name, ip in sorted(hostnames.items()):
            f.write(('%s,%s\n' % (name, ip)).encode("ASCII"))
        f.close()
        os.chmod(tmpname, 384)  # 600 in octal, 'rw-------'
        os.rename(tmpname, CACHEFILE)
        CACHE_WRITE_FAILED = False
    except (OSError, IOError):
        # Write message if we haven't yet or if we get a failure after
        # a previous success.
        if not CACHE_WRITE_FAILED:
            log("Failed to write host cache to temporary file "
                "%s and rename it to %s" % (tmpname, CACHEFILE))
            CACHE_WRITE_FAILED = True

        try:
            os.unlink(tmpname)
        except Exception:
            pass


def read_host_cache():
    """If possible, read the cache file from disk to populate hosts that
       were found in a previous sshuttle run."""
    try:
        f = open(CACHEFILE)
    except (OSError, IOError):
        _, e = sys.exc_info()[:2]
        if e.errno == errno.ENOENT:
            return
        else:
            log("Failed to read existing host cache file %s on remote host"
                % CACHEFILE)
            return
    for line in f:
        words = line.strip().split(',')
        if len(words) == 2:
            (name, ip) = words
            name = re.sub(r'[^-\w\.]', '-', name).strip()
            # Remove characters that shouldn't be in IP
            ip = re.sub(r'[^0-9.]', '', ip).strip()
            if name and ip:
                found_host(name, ip)
    f.close()
    global SHOULD_WRITE_CACHE
    if SHOULD_WRITE_CACHE:
        write_host_cache()
        SHOULD_WRITE_CACHE = False


def found_host(name, ip):
    """The provided name maps to the given IP. Add the host to the
       hostnames list, send the host to the sshuttle client via
       stdout, and write the host to the cache file.
    """
    hostname = re.sub(r'\..*', '', name)
    hostname = re.sub(r'[^-\w\.]', '_', hostname)
    if (ip.startswith('127.') or ip.startswith('255.') or
            hostname == 'localhost'):
        return

    if hostname != name:
        found_host(hostname, ip)

    global SHOULD_WRITE_CACHE
    oldip = hostnames.get(name)
    if oldip != ip:
        hostnames[name] = ip
        debug1('Found: %s: %s' % (name, ip))
        sys.stdout.write('%s,%s\n' % (name, ip))
        SHOULD_WRITE_CACHE = True


def _check_etc_hosts():
    """If possible, read /etc/hosts to find hosts."""
    filename = '/etc/hosts'
    debug2(' > Reading %s on remote host' % filename)
    try:
        for line in open(filename):
            line = re.sub(r'#.*', '', line)  # remove comments
            words = line.strip().split()
            if not words:
                continue
            ip = words[0]
            if _is_ip(ip):
                names = words[1:]
                debug3('<    %s %r' % (ip, names))
                for n in names:
                    check_host(n)
                    found_host(n, ip)
    except (OSError, IOError):
        debug1("Failed to read %s on remote host" % filename)


def _check_revdns(ip):
    """Use reverse DNS to try to get hostnames from an IP addresses."""
    debug2(' > rev: %s' % ip)
    try:
        r = socket.gethostbyaddr(ip)
        debug3('<    %s' % r[0])
        check_host(r[0])
        found_host(r[0], ip)
    except (OSError, socket.error, UnicodeError):
        # This case is expected to occur regularly.
        # debug3('<    %s gethostbyaddr failed on remote host' % ip)
        pass


def _check_dns(hostname):
    debug2(' > dns: %s' % hostname)
    try:
        ip = socket.gethostbyname(hostname)
        debug3('<    %s' % ip)
        check_host(ip)
        found_host(hostname, ip)
    except (socket.gaierror, UnicodeError):
        pass


def _check_netstat():
    debug2(' > netstat')
    argv = ['netstat', '-n']
    try:
        p = ssubprocess.Popen(argv, stdout=ssubprocess.PIPE, stderr=null,
                              env=get_env())
        content = p.stdout.read().decode("ASCII")
        p.wait()
    except OSError:
        _, e = sys.exc_info()[:2]
        log('%r failed: %r' % (argv, e))
        return

    # The same IPs may appear multiple times. Consolidate them so the
    # debug message doesn't print the same IP repeatedly.
    ip_list = []
    for ip in re.findall(r'\d+\.\d+\.\d+\.\d+', content):
        if ip not in ip_list:
            ip_list.append(ip)

    for ip in sorted(ip_list):
        debug3('<    %s' % ip)
        check_host(ip)


def check_host(hostname):
    if _is_ip(hostname):
        _enqueue(_check_revdns, hostname)
    else:
        _enqueue(_check_dns, hostname)


def _enqueue(op, *args):
    t = (op, args)
    if queue.get(t) is None:
        queue[t] = 0


def _stdin_still_ok(timeout):
    r, _, _ = select.select([sys.stdin.fileno()], [], [], timeout)
    if r:
        b = os.read(sys.stdin.fileno(), 4096)
        if not b:
            return False
    return True


def hw_main(seed_hosts, auto_hosts):
    helpers.logprefix = 'HH: '

    debug1('Starting hostwatch with Python version %s'
           % platform.python_version())

    for h in seed_hosts:
        check_host(h)

    if auto_hosts:
        read_host_cache()
        _enqueue(_check_etc_hosts)
        _enqueue(_check_netstat)
        check_host('localhost')
        check_host(socket.gethostname())

    while 1:
        now = time.time()
        # For each item in the queue
        for t, last_polled in list(queue.items()):
            (op, args) = t
            if not _stdin_still_ok(0):
                break

            # Determine if we need to run.
            maxtime = POLL_TIME
            # netstat runs more often than other jobs
            if op == _check_netstat:
                maxtime = NETSTAT_POLL_TIME

            # Check if this jobs needs to run.
            if now - last_polled > maxtime:
                queue[t] = time.time()
                op(*args)
            try:
                sys.stdout.flush()
            except IOError:

import time
import socket
import re
import select
import errno
import os
import sys
import platform

import subprocess as ssubprocess
import sshuttle.helpers as helpers
from sshuttle.helpers import log, debug1, debug2, debug3, get_env

POLL_TIME = 60 * 15
NETSTAT_POLL_TIME = 30
CACHEFILE = os.path.expanduser('~/.sshuttle.hosts')

# Have we already failed to write CACHEFILE?
CACHE_WRITE_FAILED = False

SHOULD_WRITE_CACHE = False

hostnames = {}
queue = {}
try:
    null = open(os.devnull, 'wb')
except IOError:
    _, e = sys.exc_info()[:2]
    log('warning: %s' % e)
    null = os.popen("sh -c 'while read x; do :; done'", 'wb', 4096)


def _is_ip(s):
    return re.match(r'\d+\.\d+\.\d+\.\d+$', s)


def write_host_cache():
    """If possible, write our hosts file to disk so future connections
       can reuse the hosts that we already found."""
    tmpname = '%s.%d.tmp' % (CACHEFILE, os.getpid())
    global CACHE_WRITE_FAILED
    try:
        f = open(tmpname, 'wb')
        for name, ip in sorted(hostnames.items()):
            f.write(('%s,%s\n' % (name, ip)).encode("ASCII"))
        f.close()
        os.chmod(tmpname, 384)  # 600 in octal, 'rw-------'
        os.rename(tmpname, CACHEFILE)
        CACHE_WRITE_FAILED = False
    except (OSError, IOError):
        # Write message if we haven't yet or if we get a failure after
        # a previous success.
        if not CACHE_WRITE_FAILED:
            log("Failed to write host cache to temporary file "
                "%s and rename it to %s" % (tmpname, CACHEFILE))
            CACHE_WRITE_FAILED = True

        try:
            os.unlink(tmpname)
        except Exception:
            pass


def read_host_cache():
    """If possible, read the cache file from disk to populate hosts that
       were found in a previous sshuttle run."""
    try:
        f = open(CACHEFILE)
    except (OSError, IOError):
        _, e = sys.exc_info()[:2]
        if e.errno == errno.ENOENT:
            return
        else:
            log("Failed to read existing host cache file %s on remote host"
                % CACHEFILE)
            return
    for line in f:
        words = line.strip().split(',')
        if len(words) == 2:
            (name, ip) = words
            name = re.sub(r'[^-\w\.]', '-', name).strip()
            # Remove characters that shouldn't be in IP
            ip = re.sub(r'[^0-9.]', '', ip).strip()
            if name and ip:
                found_host(name, ip)
    f.close()
    global SHOULD_WRITE_CACHE
    if SHOULD_WRITE_CACHE:
        write_host_cache()
        SHOULD_WRITE_CACHE = False


def found_host(name, ip):
    """The provided name maps to the given IP. Add the host to the
       hostnames list, send the host to the sshuttle client via
       stdout, and write the host to the cache file.
    """
    hostname = re.sub(r'\..*', '', name)
    hostname = re.sub(r'[^-\w\.]', '_', hostname)
    if (ip.startswith('127.') or ip.startswith('255.') or
            hostname == 'localhost'):
        return

    if hostname != name:
        found_host(hostname, ip)

    global SHOULD_WRITE_CACHE
    oldip = hostnames.get(name)
    if oldip != ip:
        hostnames[name] = ip
        debug1('Found: %s: %s' % (name, ip))
        sys.stdout.write('%s,%s\n' % (name, ip))
        SHOULD_WRITE_CACHE = True


def _check_etc_hosts():
    """If possible, read /etc/hosts to find hosts."""
    filename = '/etc/hosts'
    debug2(' > Reading %s on remote host' % filename)
    try:
        for line in open(filename):
            line = re.sub(r'#.*', '', line)  # remove comments
            words = line.strip().split()
            if not words:
                continue
            ip = words[0]
            if _is_ip(ip):
                names = words[1:]
                debug3('<    %s %r' % (ip, names))
                for n in names:
                    check_host(n)
                    found_host(n, ip)
    except (OSError, IOError):
        debug1("Failed to read %s on remote host" % filename)


def _check_revdns(ip):
    """Use reverse DNS to try to get hostnames from an IP addresses."""
    debug2(' > rev: %s' % ip)
    try:
        r = socket.gethostbyaddr(ip)
        debug3('<    %s' % r[0])
        check_host(r[0])
        found_host(r[0], ip)
    except (OSError, socket.error, UnicodeError):
        # This case is expected to occur regularly.
        # debug3('<    %s gethostbyaddr failed on remote host' % ip)
        pass


def _check_dns(hostname):
    debug2(' > dns: %s' % hostname)
    try:
        ip = socket.gethostbyname(hostname)
        debug3('<    %s' % ip)
        check_host(ip)
        found_host(hostname, ip)
    except (socket.gaierror, UnicodeError):
        pass


def _check_netstat():
    debug2(' > netstat')
    argv = ['netstat', '-n']
    try:
        p = ssubprocess.Popen(argv, stdout=ssubprocess.PIPE, stderr=null,
                              env=get_env())
        content = p.stdout.read().decode("ASCII")
        p.wait()
    except OSError:
        _, e = sys.exc_info()[:2]
        log('%r failed: %r' % (argv, e))
        return

    # The same IPs may appear multiple times. Consolidate them so the
    # debug message doesn't print the same IP repeatedly.
    ip_list = []
    for ip in re.findall(r'\d+\.\d+\.\d+\.\d+', content):
        if ip not in ip_list:
            ip_list.append(ip)

    for ip in sorted(ip_list):
        debug3('<    %s' % ip)
        check_host(ip)


def check_host(hostname):
    if _is_ip(hostname):
        _enqueue(_check_revdns, hostname)
    else:
        _enqueue(_check_dns, hostname)


def _enqueue(op, *args):
    t = (op, args)
    if queue.get(t) is None:
        queue[t] = 0


def _stdin_still_ok(timeout):
    r, _, _ = select.select([sys.stdin.fileno()], [], [], timeout)
    if r:
        b = os.read(sys.stdin.fileno(), 4096)
        if not b:
            return False
    return True


def hw_main(seed_hosts, auto_hosts):
    helpers.logprefix = 'HH: '

    debug1('Starting hostwatch with Python version %s'
           % platform.python_version())

    for h in seed_hosts:
        check_host(h)

    if auto_hosts:
        read_host_cache()
        _enqueue(_check_etc_hosts)
        _enqueue(_check_netstat)
        check_host('localhost')
        check_host(socket.gethostname())

    while 1:
        now = time.time()
        # For each item in the queue
        for t, last_polled in list(queue.items()):
            (op, args) = t
            if not _stdin_still_ok(0):
                break

            # Determine if we need to run.
            maxtime = POLL_TIME
            # netstat runs more often than other jobs
            if op == _check_netstat:
                maxtime = NETSTAT_POLL_TIME

            # Check if this jobs needs to run.
            if now - last_polled > maxtime:
                queue[t] = time.time()
                op(*args)
            try:
                sys.stdout.flush()
            except IOError:
                break

        # FIXME: use a smarter timeout based on oldest last_polled
        if not _stdin_still_ok(1):  # sleeps for up to 1 second
            break

But I'm sorry, I disagree with most of the proposed changes. The `fit()` methods of the estimators in `lightgbm.sklearn` should not be identical to each other...they expect different arguments for the different machine learning tasks (e.g., `group` is only relevant to ranking).

That same principle applies for the `fit()` methods on classes in `lightgbm.dask`, which are intended to mimic them. In addition, the `lightgbm.dask` estimators cannot simply have the same types for their arguments as the underlying `lightgbm.sklearn` things that they inherit from...special consideration has to be given there to how distributed training works and how to orchestrate moving data around multiple processes / machines.

If you haven't used Dask or LightGBM before, I recommend not closing this PR and not trying to attempt to fix the `mypy` warnings related to these `fit()` methods at this time.

Officially, this is the wrong way to do this. Juju should allow you to pass the local constructed model or use the existing switched model and input that into the [bundle facade to get the bundle changes](https://github.com/juju/juju/blob/develop/apiserver/facades/client/bundle/bundle.go#L257) diff.
Except that's not how the bundle facade works. Instead, it just gives you a set of changes from the existing bundle, which will always be everything.

The overall approach looks in line with what I'd been imagining as well. The only thing that looking through it raised for me is something I hadn't considered before: the separate querying and queuing by chunks of instance IDs has the potential to queue the same records multiple times within the same transfer session. Specifically, imagine this scenario:

- Device A syncs Record X to both Device B and Device C.
- Device B modifies the record, then syncs to C.
- Device C further modifies the record, then syncs back to A.
- During this last sync, imagine that the instance IDs for Device B and C are in different chunks of the FSIC.
- The `last_saved_by_conditions` for the syncing of both chunks will match Record X, and so it will be queued twice (once during each chunked query).

I don't think this should cause any catastrophic failures, but it definitely seems like something we'd want to avoid (data bloat, etc, or maybe other unforeseen consequences resulting from the expectation that this wouldn't happen). You may have other ideas for addressing it, but it may be enough to add an additional condition to the query along the lines of (warning: pseudocode) `AND store.id NOT IN (SELECT model_uuid from buffer where buffer.transfer_session_id = '{transfer_session_id}')`.

Happy to co-hack as helpful!

If we move forward with the approach, I would at least suggest to make it a bit more explicit and maybe encapsulate the decorator into a class or at least a new generator method.

I wonder though, since the log output will now be streamed like the response data, it will be jumbled through out the log output, as well, right? So I would imagine it to be fairly hard to trace in a real log file which chunks belong together. Moreover, we may be returning bytes so, in some cases, printing it may not make sense in the first place.

I was originally thinking about a really simple solution actually. For example, when we print a service response, e.g., a response to s3 GetObject, we would have the same issue, namely that we consume the S3 body stream. So instead we just print `StreamedBytes(...)` to the log.

https://github.com/localstack/localstack/blob/c882f297e5206d3b924921300b04234dae996c2f/localstack/aws/client.py#L54-L59

In this case it seems the tradeoff leans in favor of the simple solution rather than getting the output of the stream into the log. But I'd be OK with the log streaming approach as well.

This PR raises some big design questions for Pants:

1. Should this live in `check` or `lint`? According to Slack discussion led by @thejcannon and me last week, we think that the definition of `check` should likely be "lightest form of compilation, or type checking". Everything else is `lint`. So, this should be `lint` technically -- but that's annoying to run this heavy-weight thing every time you do `./pants lint ::`...
2. This runs on individual targets, and it will also leave off transitive dependencies + hashes like you mention. Really, what I think we want to run on is the _entire_ lockfile for a resolve.

The simplest way we could do this is a goal like `experimental-pip-audit`, which similar to `generate-lockfiles` does not take `Specs` and instead has a `--resolve` option (with default to all). 

But we should decide if we want pip-audit to live instead in a goal like `lint` or `check`? We could so something like "running on one target belonging to a resolve -> check the whole resolve". 

Note that others have requested things like https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/issues/15723. There is demand for a "resolve-specific lint/check interface"
